title: Nettle infusion for hay fever
subtitle: A traditional antihistamine infusion for seasonal allergic rhinitis
excerpt: A hot infusion of dried nettle leaf, taken daily during hay fever season. Nettle has a long traditional history as an antihistamine herb and a small body of clinical evidence supporting its use for mild allergic rhinitis. One of the most cost-effective self-care measures for spring and summer hay fever.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), nettle entry, anti-allergic and tonic properties. Mittman P. (1990), 'Randomized, double-blind study of freeze-dried Urtica dioica in the treatment of allergic rhinitis', small pilot trial showing 58% of participants rated nettle moderately effective for hay fever. EMA HMPC assessment of Urtica dioica (2011), traditional use for supportive treatment of joint and urinary conditions; note that the hay fever application is traditional rather than EMA-confirmed at this time.

content.text: A daily cup of nettle leaf tea during hay fever season. Nettle is the herbal tradition's response to

. Traditional herbal writers list it for its anti-allergic action, and a small clinical trial found a majority of users rated freeze-dried nettle moderately helpful for hay fever. The full mechanism is not certain, but nettle contains compounds that slow histamine release in the body. Nettle is not a replacement for antihistamine medication in severe hay fever. For mild symptoms it is a reasonable daily support, and it is rich in iron, calcium, and silica as a tonic too.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Nettle leaf at tea strength is well tolerated and has no significant drug interactions at normal doses. Dried nettle does not sting: the formic acid that causes the sting is deactivated by drying.

Blood thinners: nettle contains vitamin K, which helps the blood to clot. Very large amounts (several litres of nettle tea a day) could in theory affect INR in people on warfarin. One to three cups a day is not a worry for most people. Tell your prescriber if you are on warfarin and start taking nettle regularly.

See a medical professional for severe hay fever that is getting in the way of daily life, work, or sleep. There are very effective prescription and over-the-counter treatments. Same goes for hay fever with asthma symptoms, severe itchy eyes, or a strong reaction after allergen exposure.
attrs.title: Important safety notes

items.name: Dried nettle leaf
items.prepNote: about 1 heaped teaspoon of dried leaf

items.name: Honey
items.prepNote: optional, nettle tea is mild-tasting, grassy, and unsweet

content.text: Place {{dried-nettle-leaf}} of dried nettle leaf in the teapot or infuser. Pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over and put the lid on immediately. Steep for 10 minutes. Strain and drink. The infusion is mild-tasting, grassy and green, somewhere between spinach water and a light herb tea. Honey balances the mild bitterness if needed.

One to two cups a day through hay fever season, from the first signs of pollen until the season ends. The traditional way is to start one or two weeks before your usual hay fever start date and keep drinking it through the season, rather than waiting for symptoms to set in. For mild hay fever, this early start may keep the peak lower. For moderate or severe hay fever, use it alongside regular antihistamines, not in place of them.
When NOT to use this remedy

See a medical professional for severe hay fever that gets in the way of work, sleep, or quality of life. Do not use nettle infusion as the only management for hay fever with asthma symptoms. Asthmatic reactions to allergens need prompt medical care. Talk to your prescriber if you are on warfarin and starting regular nettle tea.
